content.title: Digitised Callback Must Enforce Time-Boxed Response with Automated Escalation
content.requirement: The digitised callback workflow must enforce a defined response window — no more than 15 minutes — and automatically escalate to a secondary contact and alert the RM if no response is received within the window, without any manual trigger.
content.rationale: Derived from TD-FRD-006. The business case for callback digitisation rests entirely on bounding the hold duration. A digital workflow without an enforced timeout replicates the open-ended nature of the current manual call and fails to address FP-FRD-001.
acceptanceCriteria: A callback challenge is delivered via at least one digital channel (email, SMS OTP or portal message) within 2 minutes of the item being flagged
If no response arrives within the configured window (default 15 minutes), the system escalates to the secondary contact and alerts the RM without manual intervention
The response window is configurable per release type without a system release
Every callback event is logged in SYS-FRD-001 with timestamp and user identity

content.title: Front Office — Upstream Drawdown Initiation
content.theDependency: The Front Office is the sole upstream initiator of funds release. Every release item begins with a drawdown request submitted by the Front Office for an approved credit facility; the process cannot start without a valid submission from this party.
content.whatCrossesTheBoundary: A drawdown request (inbound to this process) carrying the facility ID, amount, currency, value date, and beneficiary details. In the target state this crosses the boundary as a structured ISO 20022 MX message via the intake API rather than an unstructured channel submission.
content.whyItMatters: The quality of the upstream submission determines the throughput of the entire downstream process. PP-FRD-001 (incomplete submissions) and PP-FRD-003 (duplicates) are both driven by the quality of this dependency and are the primary sources of delay.

content.title: Core Banking System — Downstream Settlement
content.theDependency: The Core Banking System is the downstream settlement system that executes the actual fund movement. The process's execution step (PS-FRD-007) depends entirely on the CBS accepting and posting the approved instruction; no fund movement occurs without this system.
content.whatCrossesTheBoundary: An approved payment posting instruction (outbound from this process) carrying the held-account debit and beneficiary-account credit details. In the target state this may also include a routing flag directing qualifying sub-threshold releases to SEPA Instant rails rather than the TARGET2 batch.
content.whyItMatters: CBS unavailability or rejection results in a failed release even after all approvals are complete — the highest-risk point in the lifecycle, as the item is committed but not yet settled and cannot be reversed without a separate process.

content.title: Treasury Platform — API Availability and Integration Readiness
content.theDependency: SYS-FRD-004, the Treasury/Liquidity Platform, must expose a queryable API for intraday liquidity confirmation before the API integration in TD-FRD-005 can be implemented. This is the critical technical pre-condition for TS-FRD-003.
content.whatCrossesTheBoundary: A liquidity confirmation request — value date, release amount and currency — flows outbound from SYS-FRD-001 to SYS-FRD-004; a CONFIRMED or DECLINED availability signal flows back inbound. Both are real-time in the target state; today a manual telephone call substitutes for this boundary.
content.whyItMatters: If SYS-FRD-004 cannot expose a stable, SLA-bound API within the programme timeline, TD-FRD-005 cannot proceed and the large-value release path retains the manual desk-to-desk call, leaving FP-FRD-002 and PG-FRD-002 unresolved.

content.title: Front-Office Systems Can Produce ISO 20022 MX Within the Transition Timeline
content.theAssumption: The front-office systems used to initiate drawdown requests can generate ISO 20022 MX messages within the transition timeframe for TD-1, or a compliant legacy-channel bridge can be operated during a phased migration without introducing new data-quality failures.
content.whyItIsUnconfirmed: The front-office platform's ISO 20022 readiness has not been assessed in this session. While Fedwire and SWIFT have completed their migrations, the bank's own front-office platform may require upgrade cycles that are outside Payment Operations' control or timeline.
content.impactIfWrong: TD-1 cannot be implemented on the planned timeline; a longer legacy-channel period extends the duration during which PP-FRD-001 and PP-FRD-003 continue to affect queue throughput.

content.title: Regulatory Stance on AI Screening Remains Aligned with EBA Guidelines
content.theAssumption: The supervisory stance on AI-powered sanctions screening remains consistent with the EBA guidelines effective December 2025, and the bank's competent authority does not require an additional third-party audit or phased supervisory approval before the rules-only screening engine can be decommissioned.

content.title: Payments Workflow Tool

content.title: SWIFT / Host-to-Host File Intake

content.title: Payment Instruction Submitted for Release
content.whatTheClientDoes: The front-office team or corporate treasury enters and submits the release instruction — via the Payments Workflow Tool or an automated SWIFT/H2H file — carrying the facility ID, amount, currency, value date and beneficiary details.
content.whatTheBankDoes: The ops queue receives the item and opens it for validation — checking facility status, available limit, value date validity and document completeness.
content.experience: For the front-office team, submitting is routine handoff. For the corporate treasury client it is a moment of expectation: they have downstream obligations on the value date and are trusting the bank to deliver. Any delay in status updates breeds uncertainty.

What the bank does.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser (A2): tiered response — system alert for all holds, direct phone call for high-value or callback-required items.

Experience.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser (A4): corporate client is often unaware their payment is blocked until the RM reaches them.

content.title: Held-Payment Notification
content.whatTheClientDoes: For routine holds, the RM monitors the PWT queue and reads the system alert. For high-value or callback-flagged holds, the RM receives a direct call from the desk and must immediately begin locating the relevant contact to progress the release.
content.whatTheBankDoes: A system alert fires in the PWT queue on hold. For high-value or callback-required items, the desk also phones the RM directly to explain the hold and the required action.
content.experience: A routine hold is low-drama: an alert sits in the queue. A callback-flagged hold is high-anxiety: the RM must locate the pre-agreed contact quickly or the payment misses the value date. The corporate client is often unaware their payment is blocked until the RM calls.

What the client does.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser: RM receives PWT confirmation and relays to corporate client; client reconciles via the portal status tile.

What the bank does.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser: desk closes the workflow item and commits the audit log at step PS-FRD-008.

Experience.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser: seamless for STP items; significant relief for near-cut-off large-value releases.

content.title: Release Confirmation to RM and Client
content.whatTheClientDoes: The RM receives the confirmation notification in the PWT, relays it to the corporate treasury client, and closes the instruction in their own records. The client checks their portal status tile updating to 'released' and reconciles their position.
content.whatTheBankDoes: The desk closes the workflow item, fires the confirmation notification, and commits the audit log entry recording all actors and timestamps.
content.experience: This is the moment the service is delivered. For a clean STP item, the experience is seamless — the client's position simply changes. For a near-cut-off large-value item, this confirmation carries relief: the corporate client's downstream obligations are secured for the value date.

What the client does.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser (A5): RM receives return and communicates rejection; client must resubmit from scratch with a new reference.

What the bank does.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser: desk returns item with reason code; specifies required docs for sanctions/compliance rejections.

Experience.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser (A5): no partial-amend path; docs required for compliance rejections; frequently pushes to next business day.

content.title: Rejection / Return-for-Correction Notice
content.whatTheClientDoes: The RM receives the return notification and must communicate the rejection to the corporate treasury client. The client must then resubmit a corrected instruction — a new reference from scratch, with any required supporting documentation for sanctions or compliance-related rejections.
content.whatTheBankDoes: The desk returns the item to the front-office queue with a reason code. For documentation or sanctions-related rejections, the desk specifies the documents required before re-submission.
content.experience: Frustrating and disruptive. There is no partial-amend path — the client must start again with a new reference, losing processing priority. For compliance or sanctions rejections, gathering the required documents frequently pushes the payment to the next business day.

What the client does.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser (A3): RM reads T-60 and T-30 PWT alerts and contacts desk for pending large-value items.

What the bank does.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser (A3): PWT fires automated T-60 and T-30 alerts; desk manually nudges RM for any unreleased large-value item at T-30.

Experience.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser: RM experiences cut-off pressure; many clients unaware until they receive a slippage notification the following day.

content.title: Same-Day Cut-Off Reminder
content.whatTheClientDoes: The RM reads the T-60 and T-30 PWT alerts and assesses which large-value items remain unreleased. For items still pending, the RM contacts the desk and may relay urgency to the client if the value date is at risk.
content.whatTheBankDoes: The PWT fires automated alerts at T-60 and T-30 minutes before the 14:00 CET cut-off. The desk manually nudges the RM for any large-value item that remains unreleased at T-30.
content.experience: For the RM this is a pressure point — a countdown on items that can still miss cut-off. Many corporate clients are unaware of the clock until they receive a value-date slippage notification the following day.

The moment.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser: large-value confirmation before 14:00 CET is an explicit moment of truth SME flagged.

Why it matters.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser: corporate clients have downstream obligations (payroll, supplier payments, intercompany) contingent on value date.

content.title: Large-Value Release Confirmed Before Cut-Off
content.theMoment: The large-value release confirmation lands in the PWT and is relayed to the corporate treasury client with minutes to spare before the 14:00 CET cut-off.
content.whyItMatters: The corporate client has downstream obligations — payroll, supplier payments, intercompany transfers — contingent on funds landing on the value date. A confirmed release before cut-off means those obligations are met. A missed cut-off means overnight exposure, client apology, and damaged trust.
content.designImplication: Every operational decision — Treasury confirmation, callback resolution, approval timing — ultimately converts into this moment. The target state must make it predictable and visible, not something the RM wins or loses by luck each day.

The moment.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser (A5): desk supervisor makes the rejection call; RM communicates it to the client.

Why it matters.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser (A5): client must resubmit from scratch with no partial-amend; sanctions rejections require supporting docs before resubmission.

content.title: Held Payment Rejected — Client Must Be Told
content.theMoment: The desk supervisor calls the RM to confirm that the held payment has been rejected and cannot proceed. The RM must now make the call to the corporate treasury client.
content.whyItMatters: The corporate client is receiving news that funds they expected to move today will not. This affects their cash position, their downstream obligations, and their confidence in the bank. For sanctions-related rejections, they must also produce supporting documents before resubmission — with no partial-amend path.
content.designImplication: The rejection experience is worse than necessary — no partial-amend, no priority carry-over, no structured checklist of what is needed to resubmit. A guided rejection notice and a fast-track re-entry path for corrected items would substantially reduce client impact and RM burden.

Description.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser: ~5% of releases by volume; ~1/3 of same-day cut-off misses; contact often unreachable; adds 30–90 min.

Root cause.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser (A4): hard control, no RM override. Contact lists stale; no SMS/secondary-contact/video-callback fallback.

Client impact.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser (A4): payment held entirely; client often unaware until RM reaches them; frequently causes value-date slippage.

content.title: Manual-Release Callback Verification
content.description: Triggered on roughly 5% of releases by volume — high-value and first-use-beneficiary items — this callback requirement accounts for approximately one third of all same-day cut-off misses. The agreed contact is often unreachable, and each verification adds 30 to 90 minutes.
content.rootCause: The callback is a hard control — the RM cannot override or vouch. Contact lists are not updated when clients change treasury personnel; there is no fallback: no SMS, secondary contact or video-callback path. One stale number stalls the release.
content.clientImpact: The payment is held entirely during the verification window. The corporate client is often unaware their payment is blocked until the RM reaches them — an unexpected interruption that requires locating and confirming their own instruction. The delay frequently causes value-date slippage.

content.title: Near-Cut-Off Treasury Liquidity Confirmation
content.description: When a large-value release needs Treasury to confirm intraday funding availability and the request arrives close to the 12:00 CET cut-off, Treasury confirmation frequently lands minutes before or after cut-off — forcing value-date slippage and an immediate, difficult conversation with the RM and corporate client.
content.rootCause: The Treasury confirmation step is a manual desk-to-desk call; there is no API integration between the PWT and the Treasury/Liquidity Platform. Confirmation time is unpredictable, ranging from minutes to over an hour depending on Treasury availability.
content.clientImpact: Value-date slippage on a large release disrupts the client's liquidity planning — payroll, supplier settlements, and intercompany transfers may fail on the original date. The RM must immediately explain why approved, queued funds did not move, compounding the failure with relationship strain.

Finding.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser: Internal Audit raised this finding in 2025; same user performed both approvals in a small number of cases; HIGH; remediation in progress.

Recommendation.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser confirmed: system-level same-person enforcement is the agreed remediation direction.

content.title: 4-Eyes Same-Person Rule Not System-Enforced
content.finding: Internal Audit identified that the PWT did not enforce the same-person rule for the dual-approval control (CP-FRD-003). In a small number of cases, the same user account performed both first-line and second-line approval on the same workflow item. The control's effectiveness relies entirely on approver discipline with no automated same-person block.
content.recommendation: Implement a system-level same-person enforcement in the PWT that prevents the user identity that performed first-line approval from submitting second-line approval on the same item. The remediation plan should also address CP-FRD-003's reclassification from MANUAL to AUTOMATED once the control is system-enforced.

Finding.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser (A1): SOX/ICFR control testing raised this finding in 2025; callbacks performed but not consistently logged; control redesign agreed, retention not yet automated.

Recommendation.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser confirmed: mandatory PWT logging field before hold clearance is the agreed remediation.

content.title: Callback-Verification Evidence Not Consistently Retained
content.finding: SOX/ICFR control testing found that callback verifications for high-value and first-use-beneficiary releases were performed but not consistently logged with contact name, timestamp, and outcome. Without a complete evidential record, the callback cannot be demonstrated as executed for audit purposes. There is no mandatory PWT field requiring analysts to record callback evidence before clearing a hold.
content.recommendation: Add a mandatory PWT entry step requiring the analyst to record the contact name, timestamp, and outcome before a callback hold can be cleared. Include the retained evidence in periodic SOX/ICFR control testing and automate its capture in the workflow audit trail.

Finding.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser (A2): raised by external audit and regulatory thematic review in 2025; still open — no automated SLA monitor live; breaches detected only by manual sampling.

Recommendation.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser confirmed: automated receipt-to-posting SLA timer with daily dashboard is the required remediation.

content.title: No End-to-End Same-Day STP SLA Monitoring
content.finding: An external audit and regulatory thematic review found that the desk has no automated end-to-end measurement of the STP release cycle time against the two-hour same-business-day SLA (M-FRD-001). SLA breaches are detected only through manual sampling; systemic underperformance against the SLA could go undetected between sampling cycles, and the data needed to trend-report or root-cause cut-off misses is not captured.
content.recommendation: Implement an automated SLA timer in the PWT that records receipt-to-posting elapsed time for every release item. Produce a daily dashboard flagging breaches in real time, categorised by exception type (callback, compliance hold, liquidity deferral, cut-off miss), to support root-cause analysis and management reporting.

Finding.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser (A3): surfaced by Compliance second-line self-assessment in 2025; severity Medium; no independent review of analyst dispositions on screening hits.

Recommendation.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser confirmed: independent Compliance review above materiality threshold, with mandatory PWT fields, is the required remediation.

content.title: Sanctions False-Positive Disposition Not Independently Reviewed
content.finding: A Compliance second-line self-assessment found that Operations Analyst dispositions on potential sanctions and AML screening hits — cleared as false positives — are not subject to an independent review step before the item proceeds. A single analyst's clearance determination could release an item that a reviewer would have escalated, without any compensating detective control between the disposition and execution.
content.recommendation: Introduce an independent Compliance review for cleared potential hits above a defined threshold — all cleared hits for releases above EUR 500,000, or hits rated medium or above confidence by the screening engine. Record the reviewing officer and timestamp as mandatory PWT fields.

content.title: Incomplete or Invalid Release Request
content.description: The release request fails one or more of the four validation criteria: the facility ID is inactive, the requested amount exceeds the available undrawn limit, the value date is not a valid business day, or required supporting documents are missing.
content.handling: The Operations Analyst returns the item to the front-office queue with a reason code specifying which criterion failed. The front office must correct the defect and resubmit as a new workflow item. There is no partial-amend path — the correction requires a fresh submission reference, losing queue priority.
content.impact: The release is delayed by the return-and-resubmit cycle; same-day value is at risk if the corrected submission arrives near the cut-off. For the front-office and corporate client, the delay is unpredictable and often unnoticed until a confirmation fails to arrive.

Description.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser: confirmed sanctions/AML hit freezes item; triggers REG-FRD-001 and REG-FRD-002 reporting obligations.

Handling.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser: item frozen; desk supervisor notified; Compliance initiates freeze/reporting; does not re-enter release queue.

Impact.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser: release permanently blocked; regulatory, legal and reputational exposure severe.

content.title: Confirmed Sanctions or AML Hit
content.description: The Sanctions Screening Engine identifies a confirmed sanctions or AML match on a release item after adjudication by Compliance. The item cannot proceed to approval or execution.
content.handling: Compliance adjudicates the potential hit. On a confirmed match, the item is frozen in the PWT pending investigation; the desk supervisor is notified and Compliance initiates freeze and reporting obligations under REG-FRD-001 and REG-FRD-002. The item does not re-enter the release queue; resolution requires a separate regulatory and legal process. The RM notifies the front office and corporate client.
content.impact: The release is permanently blocked; the corporate client's funds are frozen pending investigation. The regulatory, legal and reputational exposure is severe. Value-date delivery fails and the corporate client must be informed and supported through the freeze period.

Description.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser: Treasury cannot confirm funding at PS-FRD-005 for releases at or above EUR 5M threshold.

Handling.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser (A5): deferred to next value date; re-enters at PS-FRD-005; re-screened for sanctions/AML.

Impact.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser: downstream cash-flow obligations disrupted; value-date slippage.

content.title: Insufficient Funding for Value Date — Liquidity Deferral
content.description: Treasury is unable to confirm funding availability for the requested value date for a release at or above the EUR 5,000,000 threshold. The release cannot proceed on the requested date.
content.handling: Treasury records the unavailable-funding outcome in the PWT. The desk defers the release and reschedules it to the next available value date. The deferred item re-enters at PS-FRD-005 and is re-screened for sanctions and AML before approval — the original clean-pass result does not carry through. The RM notifies the corporate client of the deferral.
content.impact: The corporate client's funds are not released on the intended value date; downstream cash-flow obligations dependent on same-day settlement may be disrupted. The settlement failure risk is mitigated by the deferral, but at the cost of value-date slippage.

Description.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser: OAF-FRD-001 confirms the system enforcement gap; ROLE-FRD-006 documents Team Lead cover-arrangement responsibility.

Handling.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser: Team Lead arranges cover; no bypass permitted; same-person detection triggers void, resubmit, and escalation.

Impact.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser: SoD failure breaches CP-FRD-003; genuine breach must be reported under REG-FRD-005.

content.title: 4-Eyes Approver Unavailable / Segregation-of-Duties Breach
content.description: The nominated Operations Approver is unavailable to provide second-line (4-eyes) approval, or — in the control weakness identified by Internal Audit — the PWT fails to prevent the first-line approver from also submitting the second-line approval.
content.handling: If the approver is absent, the Operations Team Lead arranges a cover and reassigns the item in the PWT. A different individual from the first-line approver must perform second-line approval with no bypass. If a same-person approval is detected post-facto, the item is voided, resubmitted through a full dual-approval cycle, and the incident is escalated to the supervisor.
content.impact: A segregation-of-duties failure exposes the bank to fraud and error risk and breaches CP-FRD-003. Post-facto detection delays the release further and triggers an audit trail investigation. A genuine SoD breach must be reported under governance requirements (REG-FRD-005).

Description.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser: release clears all steps but is not posted before 14:00 CET; typically Treasury confirmation or callback ran past the deadline.

Handling.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser (A5): no system auto-defer; desk manually reschedules; item re-screened on new date; RM notifies client; flagged at C-5.

Impact.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser: downstream obligations missed; RM manages client expectations; repeated misses trigger management reporting.

content.title: Missed Same-Day Cut-Off
content.description: A release that has cleared all validation, compliance and approval steps fails to be posted in the Core Banking System before the 14:00 CET same-day value cut-off, typically because Treasury confirmation or a callback verification ran up to or past the deadline.
content.handling: There is no system auto-defer — the desk manually reschedules the item to the next value date in the PWT and re-triggers the approval chain. The item is re-screened for sanctions and AML on the new date. The RM notifies the corporate client of the value-date slip. The missed cut-off is flagged at daily reconciliation (C-5).
content.impact: The corporate client's downstream obligations may not be met on the intended date; the RM must manage expectations and may need to arrange interim credit cover. Repeated missed cut-offs damage the desk's SLA record and trigger management reporting.

Description.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser (A5): PWT has no automated dedup rule; duplicates caught by analyst spot-check or only surface at end-of-day C-5 reconciliation.

Handling.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser (A5): rejected at validation with reason code if caught; if missed, surfaces at C-5 requiring trace, reversal, and incident report.

Impact.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser (A5): undetected duplicate risks double-execution, credit exposure beyond approved limit, and CBS posting error requiring reversal.

content.title: Duplicate / Double-Keyed Instruction
content.description: The same release instruction is submitted more than once — typically when the front office resubmits a returned item without referencing the original, or a SWIFT/H2H file is transmitted twice. The PWT has no automated dedup rule; duplicates are identified by analyst spot-check at validation.
content.handling: If detected at validation, the duplicate is rejected with a reason code and the front office is asked to confirm the live instruction. If it progresses undetected, the duplicate is likely to surface only at end-of-day reconciliation (C-5); both items must then be traced, any erroneous posting reversed, and the incident reported. No system-enforced unique-instruction check exists.
content.impact: An undetected duplicate could result in double-execution — releasing the same facility amount twice, causing credit exposure beyond the approved limit and a posting error requiring a CBS reversal process. Even when caught at validation, the investigation overhead is significant.

The gap.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser [Y]: client service agreements (cash-management frameworks, RFP responses) contractually commit to same-day value; no matching internal operational SLA backstops that promise.

Impact.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser [Y]: desk works best-effort against 14:00 CET cut-off; cut-off misses managed ad-hoc and invisible to governance.

content.title: Client-Facing SLA Not Backstopped by Internal End-to-End Operational SLA
content.theGap: The bank's corporate client service agreements commit to same-day value delivery. No matching internal operational SLA backstops that promise — the desk works on best-effort against the 14:00 CET cut-off, with no time-box that accounts for Treasury confirmation and callback verification.
content.impact: When Treasury confirmation or callback verification absorbs time near the cut-off, the desk cannot honour a contractual commitment it operationally never owns. Cut-off misses are managed ad-hoc and are invisible to governance, creating contractual and reputational exposure that accumulates undetected.
content.nextStep: Define an internal end-to-end operational SLA for same-day value releases that incorporates Treasury and callback time-boxes, reconcile it with the client-facing commitment, and make it a reportable metric in the desk's governance.

The gap.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser [Y]: Treasury liquidity confirmation and callback verification are both manual desk-to-desk handoffs with no system integration, tracked SLA, or fallback.

Impact.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser [Y]: no system detection of a stalled handoff; desk must investigate manually; cut-off may pass before the block is surfaced.

Next step.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser [Y]: API integration for SYS-FRD-004; digitised, time-boxed callback workflow with automated escalation on no-response.

content.title: Manual Handoff Brittleness on Critical Release Dependencies
content.theGap: The two critical dependencies — Treasury intraday-liquidity confirmation and beneficiary callback verification — are manual desk-to-desk handoffs with no system integration, no tracked SLA, and no fallback. Each is a single point of failure with no timeout, alert, or escalation path.
content.impact: Either dependency can stall a release for an indeterminate period with no system detection. There is no mechanism to surface a blocked handoff until the desk investigates manually, by which time the 14:00 CET cut-off may already have passed.
content.nextStep: Replace both handoffs with system integrations with defined time-boxes: an API call for Treasury liquidity confirmation (SYS-FRD-004) and a time-boxed, digitised callback workflow with an automated escalation path on no-response.

The gap.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser [Y]: M-FRD-001 through M-FRD-003 show "Not measured" actuals; no single role owns end-to-end flow; each step monitored in isolation.

Impact.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser [Y]: root-cause analysis of cut-off misses requires manual reconstruction; no accountability for the overall pattern.

Next step.evidence: Markus Holzhäuser [Y]: assign end-to-end ownership to Operations Team Lead; activate metrics from PWT audit log; implement SLA timer from OAF-FRD-003.

content.title: No End-to-End Cycle-Time Measurement or Process Ownership
content.theGap: Release cycle time is not measured end to end; the three documented metrics carry SLA targets but no actuals. No single role owns the end-to-end flow — each step is monitored in isolation, so systemic slippage is invisible until a cut-off miss surfaces it.
content.impact: Without end-to-end measurement, root-cause analysis of cut-off misses requires manual reconstruction. Degradation in any step propagates undetected until it becomes a client-impacting event, and the absence of a single owner means no one is accountable for the overall pattern.
content.nextStep: Assign end-to-end flow ownership to the Operations Team Lead, activate M-FRD-001 through M-FRD-003 with real measurement from the PWT audit log, and implement the automated SLA timer recommended in audit finding OAF-FRD-003.

content.title: Controlled Pilot — Large-Value Release Sub-Cohort
content.whatWeValidate: That the API-driven liquidity confirmation (TS-FRD-003), digitised callback (TS-FRD-004) and automated deferral alert (TS-FRD-006) together reduce the same-day cut-off miss rate (M-FRD-007) from ~3% toward the <1% target and push STP rate (M-FRD-005) above 65% on the pilot cohort before broader rollout.
content.howWeValidate: Run the target-state integrations in production for a defined sub-cohort of large-value releases — e.g. those above EUR 5M from a pilot set of facilities — over a 90-day observation period. Measure M-FRD-005 and M-FRD-007 against the documented baselines using the same counting method as the As-Is metrics. Compare week-by-week trend; halt if cut-off miss rate worsens.
successCriteria: Cut-off miss rate on the pilot cohort falls below 1% within the 90-day window
STP rate on the pilot cohort exceeds 65% (≥ 7-point improvement on the ~58% baseline)
No regression in genuine-match screening detection during the pilot
Learnings documented and signed off before rollout to the full release population

content.title: Shadow-Run — AI Screening Dispositions vs. Analyst Decisions
content.whatWeValidate: That the AI-powered screening layer reaches the REQ-FRD-003 threshold — ≥ 50% false-positive reduction with no genuine-match misses — before the rules-only engine is decommissioned.
content.howWeValidate: Run the AI entity-matching layer in shadow mode alongside the existing rules engine for a minimum of 60 days and 10,000 items. For each alert raised by the rules engine, record whether the AI would auto-resolve it (proposed false positive) or retain it (genuine or ambiguous hit). Compliance adjudicates all retained hits in both tracks; a review panel assesses any divergence where the AI cleared an item that Compliance would have escalated.
successCriteria: AI auto-resolves ≥ 50% of alerts the rules engine routes to Compliance, on the shadow-run population
Zero genuine hits identified by Compliance in the rules-engine track are missed by the AI layer
Shadow-run results are independently reviewed and signed off by Compliance before production cutover
Minimum 60-day observation period and 10,000-item sample completed before sign-off
